00029 
00030 #ifndef _LIB_H_
00031 #define _LIB_H_
00032 
00033 /*
00034  * Miscellaneous standard C functions for the kernel, and other widely used
00035  * kernel functions.
00036  *
00037  * Note: setjmp and longjmp are in <setjmp.h>.
00038  */
00039 
00040 
00041 #include <cdefs.h>
00042 
00043 /*
00044  * Assert macros.
00045  *
00046  * KASSERT and DEBUGASSERT are the same, except that they can be
00047  * toggled independently. DEBUGASSERT is used in places where making
00048  * checks is likely to be expensive and relatively unlikely to be
00049  * helpful.
00050  *
00051  * Note that there's also a COMPILE_ASSERT for compile-time checks;
00052  * it's in <cdefs.h>.
00053  *
00054  * Regular assertions (KASSERT) are disabled by the kernel config
00055  * option "noasserts". DEBUGASSERT could be controlled by kernel
00056  * config also, but since it's likely to be wanted only rarely during
00057  * testing there doesn't seem much point; one can just edit this file
00058  * temporarily instead.
00059  */
00060 #include "opt-noasserts.h"
00061 
00062 #if OPT_NOASSERTS
00063 #define KASSERT(expr) ((void)(expr))
00064 #else
00065 #define KASSERT(expr) \
00066         ((expr) ? (void)0 : badassert(#expr, __FILE__, __LINE__, __func__))
00067 #endif
00068 
00069 #if 1 /* no debug asserts */
00070 #define DEBUGASSERT(expr) ((void)(expr))
00071 #else
00072 #define DEBUGASSERT(expr) \
00073         ((expr) ? (void)0 : badassert(#expr, __FILE__, __LINE__, __func__))
00074 #endif
00075 
00076 /*
00077  * Bit flags for DEBUG()
00078  */
00079 #define DB_LOCORE      0x001
00080 #define DB_SYSCALL     0x002
00081 #define DB_INTERRUPT   0x004
00082 #define DB_DEVICE      0x008
00083 #define DB_THREADS     0x010
00084 #define DB_VM          0x020
00085 #define DB_EXEC        0x040
00086 #define DB_VFS         0x080
00087 #define DB_SFS         0x100
00088 #define DB_NET         0x200
00089 #define DB_NETFS       0x400
00090 #define DB_KMALLOC     0x800
00091 
00092 extern uint32_t dbflags;
00093 
00094 /*
00095  * DEBUG() is for conditionally printing debug messages to the console.
00096  *
00097  * The idea is that you put lots of lines of the form
00098  *
00099  *      DEBUG(DB_VM, "VM free pages: %u\n", free_pages);
00100  *
00101  * throughout the kernel; then you can toggle whether these messages
00102  * are printed or not at runtime by setting the value of dbflags with
00103  * the debugger.
00104  *
00105  * Unfortunately, as of this writing, there are only a very few such
00106  * messages actually present in the system yet. Feel free to add more.
00107  *
00108  * DEBUG is a varargs macro. These were added to the language in C99.
00109  */
00110 #define DEBUG(d, ...) ((dbflags & (d)) ? kprintf(__VA_ARGS__) : 0)
00111 
00112 /*
00113  * Random number generator, using the random device.
00114  *
00115  * random() returns a number between 0 and randmax() inclusive.
00116  */
00117 #define RANDOM_MAX (randmax())
00118 uint32_t randmax(void);
00119 uint32_t random(void);
00120 
00121 /*
00122  * Kernel heap memory allocation. Like malloc/free.
00123  * If out of memory, kmalloc returns NULL.
00124  */
00125 void *kmalloc(size_t size);
00126 void kfree(void *ptr);
00127 void kheap_printstats(void);
00128 
00129 /*
00130  * C string functions. 
00131  *
00132  * kstrdup is like strdup, but calls kmalloc instead of malloc.
00133  * If out of memory, it returns NULL.
00134  */
00135 size_t strlen(const char *str);
00136 int strcmp(const char *str1, const char *str2);
00137 char *strcpy(char *dest, const char *src);
00138 char *strcat(char *dest, const char *src);
00139 char *kstrdup(const char *str);
00140 char *strchr(const char *searched, int searchfor);
00141 char *strrchr(const char *searched, int searchfor);
00142 char *strtok_r(char *buf, const char *seps, char **context);
00143 
00144 void *memcpy(void *dest, const void *src, size_t len);
00145 void *memmove(void *dest, const void *src, size_t len);
00146 void bzero(void *ptr, size_t len);
00147 int atoi(const char *str);
00148 
00149 int snprintf(char *buf, size_t maxlen, const char *fmt, ...) __PF(3,4);
00150 
00151 const char *strerror(int errcode);
00152 
00153 /*
00154  * Low-level console access.
00155  *
00156  * putch_prepare and putch_complete should be called around a series
00157  * of putch() calls, if printing in polling mode is a possibility.
00158  * kprintf does this.
00159  */
00160 void putch(int ch);
00161 void putch_prepare(void);
00162 void putch_complete(void);
00163 int getch(void);
00164 void beep(void);
00165 
00166 /*
00167  * Higher-level console output.
00168  *
00169  * kprintf is like printf, only in the kernel.
00170  * panic prepends the string "panic: " to the message printed, and then
00171  * resets the system.
00172  * badassert calls panic in a way suitable for an assertion failure.
00173  * kgets is like gets, only with a buffer size argument.
00174  *
00175  * kprintf_bootstrap sets up a lock for kprintf and should be called
00176  * during boot once malloc is available and before any additional
00177  * threads are created.
00178  */
00179 int kprintf(const char *format, ...) __PF(1,2);
00180 void panic(const char *format, ...) __PF(1,2);
00181 void badassert(const char *expr, const char *file, int line, const char *func);
00182 
00183 void kgets(char *buf, size_t maxbuflen);
00184 
00185 void kprintf_bootstrap(void);
00186 
00187 /*
00188  * Other miscellaneous stuff
00189  */
00190 
00191 #define DIVROUNDUP(a,b) (((a)+(b)-1)/(b))
00192 #define ROUNDUP(a,b)    (DIVROUNDUP(a,b)*b)
00193 
00194 
00195 #endif /* _LIB_H_ */
Generated on Sun Jun 27 22:12:13 2010 for Os/161 by  1.6.1
